vitals.location: Cenes de la Vega, Granada, Spain

climate.description: Classic Sierra Nevada spring: warm valley starts (60-70°F) contrast cold high-altitude summits (30-50°F), potential snow even in May.
challenges: Precipitation: Moderate, with possible flurries at elevation.

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 77. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 73.

course_description.character: A point-to-point road route starting at the Cenes de la Vega sports centre and finishing at Pradollano ski station, around 2,100 m altitude — not at the Veleta summit itself; the separate Subida al Veleta timed climb (38 km, 2,236 m, run the following day) is a different event, not part of this Gran Fondo. The route traverses mountainous roads east of Granada through the Sierra Nevada foothills, climbing Puerto de Blancares twice — once from Quéntar, once from La Peza — before turning through Güéjar Sierra toward El Duque and Las Sabinas. 2026 course figures (119 km / 3,319 m) run materially shorter than the 146.4 km / 3,353 m previously on file; the organizer's own site is internally inconsistent, with current Spanish-language pages giving 119 km/3,319 m and older English/homepage material still showing 140 km/3,200 m or other stale figures.
course_description.signature_challenge: The dense 3,319 m of climbing packed into 119 km, capped by a classified high-mountain timed ascent on the Hotel El Duque-Las Sabinas segment (roughly 15 km, 1,177 m+) that alone decided the 2026 overall classification.
suffering_zones: Puerto de Blancares from Quéntar — approximately 7.3 km, the first of two ascents of the same pass
Puerto de Blancares from La Peza — approximately 9.4 km, the second ascent from the opposite side
Hotel El Duque-Las Sabinas — official results measured this roughly 15 km / 1,177 m+ segment (km 97.0-112.5) as the decisive classified climb

logistics.lodging_strategy: Collaborations with TODOSIERRANEVADA for discounts; Granada city offers charming hotels minutes away.
logistics.transport: Bib pickup at Polideportivo Municipal de Cenes de la Vega, bag drop, bike wash, and finish services at Pradollano. Prior editions used staggered starts for multiple distances; confirm current-year start-time logistics directly given the 2026 course is now a single 119 km route.

history.origin_story: Emblematic Spanish gran fondo showcasing Granada's cycling heritage with Gran Fondo and Subida al Veleta formats.

biased_opinion.verdict: Dense, Legitimate Climbing — But Don't Trust the Published Numbers
biased_opinion.summary: Sierra Nevada Límite is a real, well-organized gran fondo with exceptional climbing density — 3,319 m packed into just 119 km for the 2026 edition, finishing at the Pradollano ski station east of Granada. It has genuine high-mountain roads, aid stations, medical teams, bag transfer, timing and finish food for 500+ riders. The problem is the organizer's own website: the current Spanish-language pages give one set of vitals (119 km/3,319 m, Cenes-Pradollano) while stale English/homepage material shows a different, larger course (140 km/3,200 m or other retained figures) — and the finish is at Pradollano, not the Veleta summit itself, despite marketing occasionally implying otherwise. The separate Subida al Veleta (38 km, 2,236 m) run the following day is the actual timed ascent to altitude, and it's a different event from this Gran Fondo.
strengths: Exceptional climbing density — 3,319 m across just 119 km is a genuinely brutal ratio
Authentic Sierra Nevada high-mountain roads with a Pradollano ski-station finish around 2,100 m
500+ participants with aid stations, medical teams, bag transfer, timing and finish food
Two ascents of Puerto de Blancares from different sides adds real route variety to the climbing
weaknesses: The organizer's own site is internally inconsistent — current Spanish pages say 119 km/3,319 m while older English/homepage material still shows 140 km/3,200 m or other stale figures
This Gran Fondo finishes at Pradollano (~2,100 m), not the Veleta summit — the higher-altitude, higher-profile ascent is a separate event (Subida al Veleta, 38 km/2,236 m, run the following day)
Only a roughly 15 km climbing segment (Hotel El Duque-Las Sabinas) determined the 2026 classification — this was not a full-course timed race result
Riders dropped behind the safety capsule enter open traffic and must surrender their bib; there are no finish-area showers
biased_opinion.bottom_line: A legitimately hard, well-supported climbing test — just verify the current year's route on the organizer's Spanish-language pages before you go, and don't confuse it with the separate Subida al Veleta if you're after the Europe's-highest-finish bragging rights.

final_verdict.one_liner: Dense Sierra Nevada climbing test finishing at Pradollano — legitimately hard, but verify the current course, the organizer's own site disagrees with itself.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want an exceptionally dense climbing test near Granada with solid event support. If you specifically want the Veleta summit and Europe's-highest-finish bragging rights, that's the separate Subida al Veleta event, not this Gran Fondo.
final_verdict.alternatives: For the actual Veleta summit ascent, ride the Subida al Veleta (same organizer, the following day). For a comparably dense Spanish climbing fondo, look at other high-Sierra or Pyrenees gran fondos.

citations.label: Official 2026 Timing — Confirms 119 km/3,319 m Course

eligibility.notes: 2026 edition held 23 May 2026 with official timing results confirming the course; distance/elevation corrected to 119 km/3,319 m (down from 146.4 km/3,353 m previously on file) per the organizer's current Spanish-language course pages. Note the organizer's own English/homepage pages retain stale, conflicting figures. Verified 2026-07-24 via codex gpt-5.6-sol foreground research as part of the Roadie Labs editorial wave 3.
